bibliographic_data.title: Accelerating the Evolution of Convolutional Neural Networks with Node-Level Mutations and Epigenetic Weight Initialization
bibliographic_data.abstract: This paper examines three generic strategies for improving the performance of neuro-evolution techniques aimed at evolving convolutional neural networks (CNNs). These were implemented as part of the Evolutionary eXploration of Augmenting Convolutional Topologies (EXACT) algorithm. EXACT evolves arbitrary CNNs with goals of better discovering and understanding new effective architectures of CNNs for machine learning tasks and to potentially automate the process of network design and selection. The strategies examined are node-level mutation operations, epigenetic weight initialization and pooling connections. Results were gathered over the period of a month using a volunteer computing project, where over 225,000 CNNs were trained and evaluated across 16 different EXACT searches.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper introduces node-level mutation operations and epigenetic weight initialization to improve the performance of evolving CNNs using neuro-evolution techniques.
core_content.key_methodology: Node-level mutation operations, epigenetic weight initialization, and pooling connections were implemented in the EXACT algorithm to evolve arbitrary CNN architectures.
outcomes: Node mutations significantly improved evolution rates over traditional edge mutation operations.
Epigenetic weight initialization increased accuracy and generalizability of trained CNNs.
Allowing for pooling connections degraded search performance.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper investigates strategies to improve the neuro-evolution process for evolving convolutional neural networks (CNNs). The EXACT algorithm, which can evolve arbitrary CNN architectures, was used. Three key strategies were examined: node-level mutation operations, epigenetic weight initialization, and pooling connections. Over 225,000 CNNs were trained and evaluated across 16 different searches using a volunteer computing project. Node mutations improved evolution rates, while epigenetic weight initialization enhanced accuracy and generalizability. However, allowing for pooling connections reduced search performance.
